sentences.text: Samuel Lissing, they call me Sam, Lissing, L-I double-S I-N-G.
No.
No, Sam.
The 2nd of March, 1921.
I'm born Amsterdam, in Holland, the Netherlands.
Well, a very pleasant city.
And we were actually born in a very good, middle-class family.
So my childhood was very good.
My father was a musician, and my mother was a housewife.
My father played mainly the piano.
He was a teacher.
And he was a very good composer, and a lot of people came to him and asked him to write some music down.
And matter of fact, I just thought about it, that one little man used to come to him.
And-- and he come to him, and it was Joseph Schmidt, who was quite a well-known tenor in his days.
I also remember that my father was in contact with Richard Tauber.
You see, he had come when a lot of artists from other countries came to Holland.
And he was virtually in the top range of musicians.
Oh, yes.
I played.
We all-- we are very musical, the whole family.
Matter of fact, my grandfather, who was born in England, her brothers, they were from the--
No, my father's mother.
But they-- she went back to home.
She's Dutch from Dutch family.
They were a few years in England.
I don't know why.
And her brother was born there.
And all were musicians.
So my father's cousins play in London in the symphony orchestra.
And my father's brother played in Amsterdam, in Concertgebouw Orchestra.
And all were musicians, the whole family.
And so we inherited music from my family, you see.
And in Holland, my mother's father, he was in-- in the diamond trade.
So I have to learn the diamond trade.
I have to learn it.
It have to be inherited, you know?
The family have to learn.
So I had two-- I learned music, and I learned the diamond trade.
And how old were you when you first started learning music?
Eight years, seven, eight years.
I was eight years when I started.
Clarinet, always clarinet.
Because-- I don't know.
I liked it.
[LAUGHTER] Very hard to say.
My brother played the piano.
And I had to play-- but I liked the clarinet.
And of course, from clarinet I went later over to the saxophone.
You mean, the-- oh, in school first, and I had very good learn-- I got very good teachers.
You see, my teacher was-- he was a member of the symphony orchestra.
I learned classical.
And there's a little bit different than it is today.
I mean, you got 15, you start getting to know, and then we start forming bands.
And I played in all the good bands-- and made money when I was 15.
Not very much, but compared to today, it was a lot of money, yeah.
Oh, in those days big bands, we had bands of 11, 12 people in those days.
And we rehearsed day and night before we get there.
And today, it is all microphone.
And it is different.
And you know, it's [INAUDIBLE].
Call me from the Glenn Miller era-- we played the same songs in those days, and yeah.
Oh, yes.
I know-- I know where I started.
In, a matter of fact, in a hotel that is called Krasnapolsky, and still exist.
And the funny thing is I was 16.
I never forget.
And that's the first time I played in big band.
And I told my grandfather.
And he was there.
And he had a special whistle.
[LAUGHS] And while we were playing with this-- 1 o'clock at night, I heard a whistle in the hall, and I saw my grandfather sitting there.
No, in the middle of the audience, you know, to-- to actually-- that was-- in those days, we had a-- a revue, or a-- a play.
And after that was dancing, after 12 o'clock to 4 o'clock at night.
And that's the band, they start playing after the show was over, more or less.
And it was-- it was good in those days, yeah.
We lived very-- I would say-- you know about Amsterdam.
[INAUDIBLE] Amsterdam was a city of, what, 3/4 million people.
The Jewish population was-- must about 100,000, 90,000, 100,000, I can't tell you exactly.
And most Jewish people did live in two areas, in southeast and in the central.
It used to be in ghetto, you know, in the time of the inquisition.
And they start moving out.
And, well, I would say most those areas, 50%, or 40%, 50% was Jewish.
It was a very good mixture.
I'm-- I'm born in central, really.
I'm born, and then I moved to east.
Oh, I was young.
And after, I moved to south.
I mean, after the war, I moved to south.
With my father had a house in a good area in the east, a reasonable good area.
And then I want to move.
It was a house and a little garden, and we stayed there.
And, you know, like-- not like here, if you are somewhere in the area, you stay where you are for the rest of your life.
Like the green grocer, his son and his grandson, they all over there, it's the same.
That's how it was in those days.
But we had a good mixture, because like what I found out much later here-- in Poland, it was so much different between people.
In language, Yiddish, we couldn't speak Yiddish.
I didn't even know it existed.
No, we were very, very assimilated.
I had even in my family three uncles were mixed married, before I was born, really.
Fortunate, in a way, because they survived the war.
You see, they-- and they had no children.
But no, we had no-- no hardship.
And that was our downfall, really, because we never took it serious.
Holland was not in the first war.
They were neutral.
And we never took that serious, like what we read in the papers in those days, that they would come to Holland.
They never gave it a second thought.
It was discussed.
I had even family in England.
You know, far family, and I easy could have gone to England.
I even had in, when I finished school, I played in a band.
And accidentally was in Belgian, band, and I was 18, 19-- 19 years, turning 19.
And that band went from Holland to Switzerland end of 1939.
I could have gone with them.
I would have been out of the war.
And my mother thought I was too young, and I stayed home.
No, because it was not-- no.
Like I said, we never expect anything.
We didn't believe it, what you read in the paper, you know?
And-- and of course, we had anti-Semitism, but different.
No, never, never.
I tell you-- I'll give you one-- one-- I've told it many, many times.
I had one friend, and he played in my band.
And he played the trombone.
He was a very tall fellow.
And as it happened, his name was Leo Frankfurt.
Frankfurt was a little bit of a Jewish name, really.
And we used to rehearse in my place, or in his place, or elsewhere, you know.
We went to all the-- it was very noisy, all those guys.
We never always at halls.
And one day Leo came to me in, I think it was '41.
And he said, listen, Sam, you have to go.
My father wants to talk to you.
And I looked at him, and said, why?
What do you mean?
He said, look, it's getting dangerous.
You know, it's started, in-- And I said, what about you?
He said, I am not Jewish.
I said, you're not Jewish?
He said, no.
He said, I'm Catholic.
You're Catholic?
I said, I didn't know.
He said, you never ask.
You understand?
That was the difference that we had.
Another thing I have to tell about Holland that when the Germans came in '40, nothing happened the first three, four months-- nothing, And--
Yeah.
Oh, yeah, yeah.
That's before the war.
Oh yeah, I'm going too far, yes.
No, before the war, and my grandfather was in the diamond industry.
You know, was in small diamond merchant.
And he felt that I should learn the trade.
So I went there.
I was still in school.
And I've learned--
Oh, I was 17.
And I've learned the diamond cutting.
And, yes, I-- I was a diamond cutter.
Oh, not-- I want to be musician, not a diamond cutter, really.
But I benefit-- I'm alive because of diamond cutting, really, because they-- after, we had a diamond spare.
Yeah, we did.
But before the war years, I-- I-- we had a good life.
You see, my farther was a good, middle-class family.
And we had no hardship.
No, no, nothing.
We were very-- my father was very liberal, very liberal.
To be honest, I knew nothing about any.
I never had any-- any Jewish upbringing at all.
We were there.
Nothing.
We part of the nation.
You can be Catholic, you can be Christian, you can be-- and that's it.
Because my parents were not really very much-- well, my father-- we had the-- in Amsterdam, Jewish people, they eat matzohs in Pesach.
And they do Jewish New Year.
But that was about all.
We never went to synagogues.
We have weddings and funerals.
No, forgot about it.
[LAUGHS] You know, that was be honest.
I mean, people say, yes, but no.
It was not the money, because we were not-- we weren't wealthy, with people say, yes, they could not afford to be bar mitzvahed.
You know, it was expensive.
No, it was not in it.
None of my cousins.
And that's it.
Why?
I can't explain.
I learned French in school and not Hebrew.
You see?
And then I keep saying, well, that's it.
So did you have any Jewish education at all?
No.
I can't read one word.
Kosher, that's all.
I know what it means.
[LAUGHTER] Nothing.
And then-- and after the war, I've never being interested for-- my reasons.
It's my reason, you know, too.
You know?
And--
Yeah?
Very well, we mixed very well.
There was absolutely no-- in school, in high school, we had absolutely no difference.
I had good friends.
And I got good memories of all of them.
And yes, no, I would say we had a good life there.
We were very fortunate.
Good.
What do you mean, how?
No, I was-- I don't know.
I was young.
I was still in school.
I mean, I was never really in it.
I was only part-time all the time because I was still in school, and then the war started.
And it was over then.
No, I could read very good music.
And-- and I had no trouble to play anywhere, you know, to-- to play four or five men.
And they say, can you read it?
Yes, and then you follow.
And it's different with today, of course.
We were much better musicians in those days because we could read music.
We learned to read music.
No, that's good life, yes.
And then we had a good life there, this very pleasant family.
Oh, my brother.
My little brother.
My father was too good for us, you know.
He was more classical, you know.
And then-- no, we are sometimes, we could force him to the piano and play a little bit with us, you know, reluctant.
And my mother played.
She was not bad, yeah.
And I met even-- my father had an-- [INAUDIBLE] pupil from conservatorium.
He gave him extra lessons.
I met him here-- he was a conductor on the Sydney Symphony Orchestra.
William from Waterloo.
He died many years ago.
I didn't know, but he knew my name, and I met him because my friend, he was a consul.
My school friend became consul here after the war.
And I met him by accident back here.
So our before the war life, in all, was good.
After, there was a lot of change, not the same anymore, you know.
The whole world have changed, you know.
And we traveled a lot.
I have family in Belgium.
I had family in England.
We went to England.
You know, we traveled quite a bit for young people in those days.
And then-- and yes, I've seen the world.
I mean, if you see the world, that means you could go 100 kilometers, you've seen the world in those days, because the world is around us.
Australia-- never heard of Australia.
And that's it.
Being a musician.
Oh, modern, all modern music, yeah, yes.
I, matter of fact, I had some lessons accidentally from a saxophone player who was there, who was one of the best in the world, Coleman Hawkins.
He was-- now, no one heard of him now.
A musician will know.
And he was in Amsterdam, and he played there.
And it was a good-- god city for jazz in those days, for modern music.
And I approached him, and he gave me a few lessons.
It was good.
No, I was playing.
If you play clarinet, you can play saxophone.
But if you play saxophone, you can't play clarinet.
People don't know.
It's a different instrument.
Who?
Coleman?
No, I went there.
No, he was playing in the-- in the-- [NON-ENGLISH] bar there.
And I walked inside, and listened to him, and started to talk to him.
He was negro, a black fellow, an American.
And I asked him-- his technique was fantastic.
And he said, well, you come, and I teach you.
No money.
You play?
Oh, yes.
This was maybe 1936, '35, '36.
I was 15, 16, yeah.
Wow.
Good old memories, good old days, yeah, a long time ago now, yeah.
But like I say, we had no hardship, no war, no problems.
We were out of the war.
People, like Jewish people in Holland were poor, average.
But they lived well.
You know, poor, but enough to eat.
No money-- live from week to week.
No demands, no motorcars, push bikes, dancing on Saturday, a little money, you know.
The boys and girls went out, and they--
Yes, yeah, always had a girlfriend.
And I met a girl in 1940, '41-- '40.
No, it would be '40.
And we got engaged.
Sophia.
And--
In the dancing and playing music.
[LAUGHS] I have to tell something, meant to tell about it.
I had a friend, and I liked that girl.
And I went to her.
She was, you know, very shy little girls in those days.
And I went to her table.
I say, can I blah, blah, blah, talk?
She wasn't interested.
And the second time I went again, I said, oh, no.
You can't take me for a cup of coffee because I got my sister is with me.
I said, it doesn't matter.
I got my friend here.
They 55 years married now.
[LAUGHTER] And I ring them every month.
They're both alive, yeah.
But in-- because of my grandfather-- I have come back to the war.
Because of my grandfather, I got a diamond spare.
The Germans picked out, I think, 200 diamond cutters.
And they gave them a spare that they could work.
They could stay behind.
It was all a trick, what we find later.
And--
The Germans, you know, the Germans they all--
--they'd always make spares, and always give you something.
They'd say, [SPEAKING GERMAN]-- something like that.
If you have that spare, that stamp on your identity card.
And we felt we were pretty safe.
[SPEAKING GERMAN]-- that is, temporarily free of work in Germany.
That was system of the-- of the [INAUDIBLE] that we parted again.
And we were told, if you get married, you stay together regardless what happen.
So all those young boys and girls, they all got married.
Yes, we all got married in 20, 30 at one, in 10 minutes.
There was no religious wedding, only registered.
And so that's-- a matter of fact, there's one couple here.
They're married for 52 years.
They married the same day as I married.
My brother and his wife, they're married the same day.
We all married the same day-- the 1st of April, 1942.
It wasn't real.
I mean, you're not real married.
You know, we were too young to get married.
And it was like force.
What I remember when we came home, and then said to mother, now, we are married. and they say, yes, but you can't stay together because-- but we did.
Oh, yes.
We did.
But we-- it was not real for them.
And--
No, it starts changing already in '41.
In 1941, there was in Holland, in Amsterdam, an organization that-- look the Dutch Nazis started.
But they were very minor.
They were more or less hoodlums, and bred Nazis, for money.
And they start already throwing in windows [BACKGROUND NOISE] bricks.
Hm.
We'll just keep going.
It's cool.
Don't worry.
They're throwing bricks in windows and Jewish boys, and they say, communist.
They started together fighting the Nazis.
And they had a march in the city in Amsterdam.
It is well known that.
And--
No, I wasn't there at all.
Jewish no, they're the Nazis.
The Dutch, the Dutch Nazis marching and start a fight.
And in that fight, one Nazi was killed.
You've heard about it?
He was now killed in Amsterdam.
Koot, I will never forget his name.
And the Germans never interfered till that day.
And it happened one week.
And I wasn't there, fortunate.
Amsterdam is surrounded by canals and water.
They-- they lifted all the bridges.
And they picked from the street 2,000 boys-- to-- to select 200.
How to find out?
You always drop your pants.
All were circumcised, and I-- And the 200 boys, they were sent to a camp in Holland one day.
And the next day, the Monday, the whole city was on strike.
And it's a well-known strike, the February strike.
I was living in the other part of the city.
We knew what happened after.
From those boys, one came back.
They were sent to Mauthausen, and they all were killed, one after the other one.
And that was the beginning.
Oh, we heard it straightaway.
I mean, it was immediately it went around.
The Germans started.
And then we started getting frightened.
That is the time, '41.
Then it really start getting bad.
And--
Yeah, fright, getting away.
But where could we go?
It was very-- look, we didn't have the right contact.
It's very hard to get away.
And all the contacts were not so good because, say, 100 escape, and 90 were picked up.
And a lot of-- yeah, some of them came to Belgium, Switzerland, and unoccupied France, but very few.
I don't know many of them who really escaped.
I didn't have to, you see, in the beginning.
And then I had the diamond spare, and that kept me-- kept me sitting there in Holland.
That was maybe my-- my-- well, my spare.
I was spared.
I was free for a while.
I was not a political, I was not--
To go underground, no, it was too dangerous for us.
You know, being Jewish and being in underground, and where can we go to?
We didn't have the right contacts.
So how did it feel being sort of separated as being Jewish when it hadn't really been a part of your life up till then?
Well, we were not real-- we were not real separated with the-- what they've done in Holland, all the Jews outside of Amsterdam have to come to Amsterdam.
And we stayed in our area because they were, more or less, it was more or less 50% Jewish area.
But the shops were closed for us.
We only could shop between 2:00 and 5:00.
But the people very good to us in that way.
And then we had to wear our--
Well, the shops there, they supplied everything what they had for us.
They don't-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how can I say-- we didn't-- we were not sure of anything.
Everything what was required, we got from the shops.
So but we didn't-- how can I say-- we didn't feel it.
You know, food wise or anything or between 2:00 and 5:00 was not very important.
We could buy our-- what we needed, we could buy.
But we couldn't go on the street after.
That became in '42-- we couldn't go on the street after 8 o'clock.
It was all-- all the time something more.
New regulations came up, all the time more regulations.
No, then, I-- yeah, we played amongst ourselves, yes.
Oh, yes, we had some Jewish-- the Jews could go to certain establishments.
May be danger, but you realize maybe danger.
They could have picked up the whole all in one go.
That's how my father was picked up.
He was working somewhere in one of the suburbs outside, in a hall.
And that day, they round the whole lot up.
No, playing there.
In '42, end of '42.
I don't know.
We know they were picked up.
That's it.
Never heard of him since.
And my mother, they came at home for my mother.
And we told her, no.
We say, she have to look after us, because I got my diamond spare.
And they kept her.
I was shocked.
I mean, it was-- but he was not the only one that left around-- around us.
It started in '42.
You see, in 19-- the first Jewish people in Holland were picked up.
It must have been June, May, June, '42.
And they start rolling them up more and more.
People were in panic.
I was home.
I don't know.
It was shock.
My father was a very good man, you know.
And then--
Oh, yes.
We knew it straightaway because the news, they worked very fast, yeah.
We knew it in two hours later.
[INAUDIBLE] picked up.
They're gone.
Where to?
I don't know.
Don't know.
Camp?
Westerbork?
Nothing.
I knew nothing about the camps till I was in it.
Nothing.
Oh, very, very bad.
My mother they did-- I can tell a lot of it later when my mother came to in camp where I was for a while.
She insisted going there.
More it was voluntary, she went there.
In Holland, in camp.
I was in camp in Holland.
But till--
Oh, yes.
We had to wear a star.
But that didn't bother us.
You know, in the city, there was no one bothers you to.
I walked without star too many times, you know.
It was-- oh, yes, it was dangerous to.
I even had to go to outside Amsterdam.
I had to, but no star.
I went in the train, and then--
No, I wasn't.
You see, I didn't look real Jewish.
I mean, obviously looking Jewish.
We were not outspoken.
Our-- our accent was the same.
We had no-- no-- no-- no one else would know I have a Jewish accent.
It was not existing.
We spoke Amsterdam, more or less there.
No-- you see, like in many countries, they have the second language, and they could pick out that they came from somewhere.
But no, we didn't have it.
And--
